comments:
Summary:
This paper presents an Ant Colony System (ACS) algorithm for the Dynamic Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (DVRPTW). The main contributions are (1) a joint solution-construction mechanism in which vehicle and next customer are selected simultaneously during state transitions, (2) hybridization with local search (relocate and exchange) and an insertion heuristic to reduce vehicle count, and (3) an experimental evaluation on a benchmark derived from Solomon instances with varying dynamicity levels, compared to a published ACO solver (MACS-DVRPTW). The authors provide source code and instances for reproducibility and report statistical tests over 30 runs.

Strengths:
- The algorithmic design is clearly described and grounded in established ACS methodology; the joint vehicle+customer selection and the use of a strong insertion heuristic are sensible innovations tailored to the dynamic setting.
- The experimental setup is thorough: 36 dynamic instances at several dynamicity levels, 30 runs per instance, use of the original MACS-DVRPTW code for fair comparison, and two statistical tests are reported.
- Results show that DVRPTW-ACS is competitive and particularly advantageous on higher-dynamicity instances; the authors discuss reasons (simultaneous construction, insertion heuristic) and acknowledge computational-cost differences (Java vs C).
- Source code and instances are made available, improving reproducibility.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
- Comparison scope: the paper compares mainly with one prior ACO-based solver (MACS-DVRPTW). To strengthen the empirical claims, include baselines beyond that single ACO variant — e.g., state-of-the-art non-ACO DVRPTW or static VRPTW heuristics adapted to the dynamic case — or at least discuss more broadly why MACS-DVRPTW is the most relevant comparison.
- Ablation analysis: the paper attributes gains to the joint selection mechanism and the insertion/local-search components. An ablation study (turning off joint selection or insertion/local search) would help quantify the contribution of each part.
- Runtime reporting: the paper notes that MACS-DVRPTW produced more feasible solutions per run in part due to implementation language differences. Provide normalized CPU-time comparisons or report wall-clock runtimes and cost-per-solution metrics so readers can judge practical trade-offs between solution quality and execution cost.
- Statistical reporting: the authors state that two tests were used and indicate where differences are significant; report p-values or significance thresholds, and indicate which test was used in ambiguous cases.
- Generality/limitations: clarify how sensitive results are to parameter choices (q0, alpha, beta, rho, candidate list size) and whether the approach scales to larger instances (more customers) or more complex dynamic settings (e.g., dynamic travel times, priorities).

Presentation and clarity:
- The paper is well organized and readable. A few minor typographical/formatting artifacts appear in the provided preprint but do not obscure the content. Figures/tables referenced (e.g., boxplots, tables) are informative; ensure all are legible in final version.

Recommendation:
Overall, this is a solid, well-motivated contribution that advances ACO application to DVRPTW, with convincing empirical evidence that the proposed approach handles higher dynamicity well. I recommend acceptance after minor revisions that address broader comparisons, ablation or runtime results, and clearer statistical reporting.
